howItWorks.intro: This property tax calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Annual Property Tax = Assessed Value × Tax Rate
Assessed Value = Home Value × Assessment Ratio
National average effective property tax rate: ~1.10%
howItWorks.outro: Real estate values and regulations vary significantly by location. Work with a local agent or attorney.

faq.question: Which state has the highest property tax?
faq.answer: New Jersey consistently has the highest effective property tax rate at approximately 2.23%, followed by Illinois at 2.07% and Connecticut at 2.15%. On a $350,000 home, New Jersey property taxes would cost roughly $7,800 per year compared to about $980 in Hawaii, which has the lowest rate at 0.28%. These rates are averages and can vary significantly by county and municipality.

faq.question: How is property tax assessed?
faq.answer: Your county assessor determines your home's assessed value, which may differ from market value depending on your state's assessment ratio. Some states assess at full market value (100%), while others use a fraction. Tax bills equal the assessed value multiplied by the local mill rate (tax rate). Reassessments happen on varying schedules — some states reassess annually, others every few years.

faq.question: Can I appeal my property tax assessment?
faq.answer: Yes, homeowners can appeal if they believe their assessed value is too high. Start by reviewing your assessment notice and comparing with recent sales of similar homes. File a formal appeal with your county assessor or board of review within the deadline (usually 30-90 days after notice). About 30-40% of appeals result in a reduction, so it is often worth pursuing if you have evidence of overvaluation.

faq.question: Are property taxes tax deductible?
faq.answer: Yes, property taxes are deductible on federal income tax returns if you itemize deductions. However, the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act capped the State and Local Tax (SALT) deduction at $10,000 total ($5,000 if married filing separately). This cap includes both state income tax and property tax combined, which limits the benefit for homeowners in high-tax states.

faq.question: Do property taxes go up every year?
faq.answer: In most states, property taxes can increase annually as home values rise and local governments adjust mill rates. Some states have caps — California Proposition 13 limits annual increases to 2% of assessed value until the home is sold. Texas has a 10% annual cap on homestead appraisal increases. Without such caps, taxes can jump significantly in hot housing markets.
